text: The mechanism of the homolytic cleavage of carbon–halogen bonds in copper-catalysed cross-coupling reactions remains unclear due to the complexity of copper redox processes and the instability of the highly reactive cuprate species involved. Here, we show that α-haloacetonitriles react with both ionic and neutral copper(I) species in an oxidative addition manner to give the first examples of alkyl copper(III) species. These cuprate species were fully characterised and were shown to be remarkably stable due to a strong Cu–CF₃ bond and a high activation energy barrier for the C(CF₃)–C(CH₂CN) reductive elimination process. A detailed mechanistic study based on kinetic and density functional theory (DFT) calculations showed that oxidative addition occurs by two different routes, depending on the copper(I) species used: the ionic species undergoes an Sₙ2 reaction analogue and the neutral species reacts via a halogen-atom transfer (XAT) mechanism. Ligand acceleration of the neutral copper(I) species was observed, a phenomenon that is also present in the activation of alkyl electrophiles in copper-catalysed cross-coupling reactions of amines, azoles and alkynes.
explanation: Extremely domain-specific and well-structured, characteristic of high-quality scientific writing; such seamless, technical prose can be produced by AI as well as by humans, though the evenness of detail slightly leans toward AI-generated text.
